summary: McKenzie describes squashing a bug in Bingo Card Creator after spending hours trying to find a clean solution. He eventually resorted to a dirty hack that touched only 2 lines instead of the clean solution's six files. The impact was minimal, affecting only 8 out of 560 word lists and only noticeable with non-default settings. He accepts the pragmatic tradeoff and commits to shipping replacement CDs to six affected customers.
key_points: The 'clean' solution required touching six files while the 'dirty' hack only needed 2 lines changed
Pragmatic shipping decisions sometimes trump architectural purity, especially when customer impact is minimal
Slacking on testing has real costs: he had to mail replacement CDs to six customers who received buggy copies
money_quotes: After hours of fruitlessly trying to solve the problem in some clean way, I eventually dirty hacked my way around it.
Ironically, the "clean" solution required touching six files, the "dirty" solution required touching 2 lines.
I think I'll chalk this one up to "Eh, sometimes you just have to ship it."
Now to pay my penance for slackadaisical testing earlier: new CDs for six people, who each got one with the bug on it.
key_insight: Sometimes the 'dirty' two-line fix that ships is better than the 'clean' six-file refactor that doesn't.
